title: Stale integer literal for prefix length after Phase 4 'cuda:' → 'npu:' string replacement
problem_description: Phase 4 rule migration mechanically replaces the string 'cuda:' with 'npu:' in code, but does NOT update integer literals that encode the OLD prefix length (5 characters for 'cuda:'). In config.py:242, a field validator used v[5:] to strip the device prefix before checking isdigit(). After migration, 'npu:1' was processed as v[5:] = ':1' (wrong — should be '1'), causing isdigit() to reject valid NPU device identifiers. This manifested as the layout_detector failing to recognize NPU device configurations.
rough_fix_approach: After Phase 4 rule migration, scan for string-slice operations whose integer arguments correspond to the length of replaced device-prefix strings. In this case: v[5:] → v[4:] to match the 4-character 'npu:' prefix. The general detection rule: find integer literals == 5 near code that previously referenced 'cuda:' (both in string literals and function args like torch.device(), device=, .to()). Those 5s likely encode the cuda: prefix length and need to be reduced to 4 after migration.
artifact_evidence: validated/phase_5_validation_canonical.json:lines 269-278 (review_verdict and review_gate explaining the v[5:]→v[4:] fix in detail)
validated/phase_35_static_validate_canonical.json (mentions off-by-one string slice fix at config.py:242 confirmed by 135/157 tests passing)
glmocr/config.py:line 242 (actual code: v[4:] — the post-fix state)

involved_code_files.role: Device configuration class — _validate_device field_validator uses string slicing to extract device index from 'npu:N' identifier
Layout detector — calls _validate_device and routes device selection (NPU vs CPU vs explicit config)

reasoning: This is a structural limitation of the Phase 4 rule migration approach: string replacement is purely text-based and does not understand string-length semantics. Any code that uses a length-dependent integer literal (like a slice index) tied to the 'cuda:' prefix will silently break. This pattern can occur in device validation, tensor shape calculations, memory offset computations, or any code that parsed CUDA device strings. A post-Phase-4 scan for integer literals equal to len('cuda:')=5 near device-related code could catch many instances. Confidence is 0.85 because while the pattern is generalizable, not every project will have such length-dependent code — it depends on whether the project does explicit string parsing of device identifiers.
